AOs to observe diligently the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 specifically on the handling, utilization, and liquidation of CAs; and
MA to maintain and enhance MGB’s monitoring reports to effectively track the handling of CAs. This will also aid in determining whether demands for liquidation should be issued for CAs that have remained outstanding for an extended period.

We recommended and Management agreed to rigorously maximize the utilization of the 20% DF and ensure the timely implementation of PPAs to attain the desired social, economic, and environmental outcomes crucial for improving quality of life.
We also recommended and Management agreed to religiously transfer the 20% of the NTA to the corresponding bank account to ensure availability of funds for the implementation of the 20% DF priority PPAs.
We likewise reiterated our prior year’s recommendation and the Municipal Planning and Development Council agreed to convene and endorse the realignment of the unexpended balances from completed 20% DF PPAs to other priority development PPAs.

We recommended and Management agreed to ensure:
Strict adherence with COA Circular No. 2012-002 and other the relevant provisions in the preparation of LDRRMFIP. Moreover, to ensure that the planned PPAs for the year are detailed in the LDRRMFIP.
That only those expenditures related to the implementation of DRRM PPAs should be charged against the LDRRMF. Also, strictly adhere to the guidelines set forth under NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1 and other applicable laws, rules, and regulations governing the utilization of the LDRRMF.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the MA, MT, and Human Resource and Management Officer to maintain and enhance their close coordination in thoroughly reviewing the schedule of amounts for remittance. This is to prevent over-remittance and under-remittance and ultimately eliminate discrepancies. Likewise, to ensure that all taxes withheld are fully remitted in the month following the withholding.

We commend MGB for its efficient delivery of priority PPAs aimed at disaster risk reduction and therefore alleviating possible adverse effects of calamities.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to:
Require the Municipal Engineer to provide the Inventory Committee with complete description and cost segregation of road components to facilitate the preparation of the RPCRNS in the prescribed formats as provided under COA Circular No. 2015-008; and
Require the MGSO to maintain a Road Map and prepare and submit the necessary RLRN in the form prescribed by COA Circular No. 2015-008.
We likewise recommended that the MAO and the MGSO maintain separate subsidiary records/LRNLCs and LRNPCs, respectively, for local road projects to ensure proper recording and control of the assets.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to:
Require the MGSO and the MAO to prioritize the reconciliation process and ensure that discrepancies noted are immediately investigated and adjusted in the books accordingly;
Require the MAO to maintain complete and updated PPELCs to support GL account balances and the MGSO to keep corresponding PCs for control purposes; and
Require the MGSO to conduct physical inventory and inspection of all unserviceable and obsolete items so that these are properly disposed of and dropped from the books of accounts in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular Nos. 89-296 and 92-386.

We recommended and the LCE direct the BAC, its Secretariat and Technical Working Group (TWG) to:
Abide strictly with the standards set forth by RA No. 9184, its 2016 Revised IRR and other relevant issuances on government procurement;
Exercise due diligence in the performance of duties to ensure that all requirements of the procurement law are faithfully adhered to particularly during post-qualification of bidders; and
Undergo relevant trainings on government procurement that will enhance their capabilities in discharging their respective functions in an effective and efficient manner.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the MEO to facilitate the completion of the Bulusan Resettlement Project through constant monitoring and coordination with contractor.
For future procurement of infrastructure projects, we also recommended and the MEO agreed to carefully carry out a feasibility/preliminary engineering works and detailed engineering to ensure that issues such as technical viability of the project, road right of way, relocation of informal settlers and coordination with other government agencies relative to the issuance of required permits/clearances, are properly addressed to ensure smooth implementation of projects.

We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendations and the LCE agreed to:
Require the MA to ensure proper accounting of the utilization of fund transfers received from NGAs through accurate recording of transactions and proper maintenance of SLs and supporting schedules;
Direct the MA to revisit the non-moving unexpended balances of fund transfers received from various NGAs and thereafter return the balances to the NGAs if still identifiable. Otherwise, make representations and obtain authority from concerned SAs to utilize the unexpended balances for purposes other than what are provided in the Memorandum of Agreement. Also, unexpended account balances of fund transfers that are completed but not refunded because of undetermined SAs shall be returned/remitted to the Bureau of Treasury; and
Require the MA to investigate and determine the causes of the negative SL balances and effect the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct balances thereof.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the MA to recognize the balance of uncollected concessionaire’s fee as receivables and revenue at the end of each year.
Moreover, we recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the BLGU-WS Administrator to come up with strategies in improving further the income of the waterworks system operations. Particularly, we suggested the following measures:
Intensify the collection of concessionaires fee through strict implementation of Municipal Ordinance No. 1, series of 2009; and
Conduct a comprehensive study on BLGU-WS’ operations to identify weaknesses in the collection system and device strategies to encourage consumers to pay concessionaires fees to generate additional income.

We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendations and Management agreed to comply strictly with DILG and DBM JMC No. 2017-1, by taking into consideration the following:
Develop a concrete plan that will articulate the PPAs, timelines, expected deliverables for each phase, key milestones, etc.; and
Adopt strategies to maximize the utilization of the 20% DF and implement the PPAs within the targeted timeframe to achieve the social, economic and environmental outcomes essential in attaining the constitutional objective of quality of life for all.

We also recommended and the LCE agreed to require the MA instead to facilitate the reconciliation of prior years’ withheld taxes with the amounts remitted to determine the actual amounts due to BIR. Thereafter, prepare the necessary adjusting entry to correct the Due to BIR account.

We also recommended and the LCE agreed to require the MA to conduct detailed review and analysis of the un-reconciled balances in the inter-agency payable accounts, including confirmation with the respective GOCCs, to determine the correct balance of the said accounts and thereafter, effect the necessary adjustments/corrections in the books.

We recommended that Management:
Comply strictly with the requirements of RA No. 9003 particularly on the efficient collection, proper transfer and transport of wastes; efficient management of residuals; adoption of modern technologies for the destruction/reuse of residual wastes; and competent operation of final disposal sites; and
Prioritize provision/allocation of enough budget for full implementation of SWM PPAs to address all the identified key issues as provided in the Municipal SWM Plan.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendations and Management agreed to:
Require the MGSO and the Office of the Municipal Accountant to prioritize the reconciliation process and ensure that discrepancies noted are immediately investigated and adjusted in the books accordingly;
Require the Office of the Municipal Accountant to maintain complete and updated PPELCs to support GL account balances and the MGSO to keep corresponding PCs for control purposes; and
Require the MGSO to conduct physical inventory and inspection of all unserviceable and obsolete items so that they may be properly disposed of and dropped from the books of accounts in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular Nos. 89-296 and 92-386.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Direct the MT and/or Supply Officer to conduct a physical count of all inventory items semestrally and render a report thereon within the prescribed period;
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to properly account for the acquisition and issuance of the supplies and materials inventory based on IPSAS 12, Inventories, and make the necessary adjustments to reflect the correct balance of the affected inventory and expense accounts; and
Require the MT and/or Supply Officer and the Municipal Accountant to properly maintain and update the stock cards and SLCs, respectively, and reconcile their records regularly.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Require the Municipal Engineer to provide the Inventory Committee with complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects to facilitate the preparation of RPCLRN;
Direct the Inventory Committee and MGSO to prepare and submit the RPCLRN and RLRN, respectively, in accordance with the prescribed formats as provided under COA Circular No. 2015-008; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to disclose the total road network in the Notes to the FS in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2015-008.
We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant and the MGSO agreed to prepare and maintain separate subsidiary records/LRNLCs and LRNPCs, respectively, for local road projects to ensure proper recording and control of the assets.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant and the MT to prioritize the reconciliation of the Cash in Bank accounts in order to come up with the correct balancesof the said accounts as presented in the FS;
Direct the Municipal Accountant to take up the unrecorded deposits, disbursements and all valid reconciling items by preparing JEVs duly supported with proper documentation and furnish the Audit Team with copies of the same; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to conduct a thorough review of records to determine the validity of long outstanding deposits in transit and stale checksand make the necessary adjustments.

We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendations and Management agreed to strictly comply with DILG and DBM JMC No. 2017-1, by taking into consideration the following:
Develop a concrete plan that will articulate the PPAs, timeline, expected deliverables for each phase, key milestones, etc.; and
Adopt strategies to maximize the utilization of the 20 per cent DF and implement the PPAs within the targeted timeframe to achieve the social, economic and environmental outcomes essential in the attainment of the constitutional objective of a desired quality of life for all.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure proper accounting of the utilization of fund transfers received from NGAs through accurate recording of transactions and proper maintenance of SLs and supporting schedules;
Revisit the non-moving unexpended balances of fund transfers received from various NGAs and thereafter return the balances to the NGAs if still identifiable or make representations and obtain authority from them to utilize the unexpended balances for purposes other than what are provided in the Memorandum of Agreement; and
Request for write-off of balances which remain non-moving for 10 years or more and where settlement could no longer be ascertained, in accordance with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to conduct a detailed review and analysis of the inter-agency payable accounts, including confirmation with the respective government agencies, to determine the correct balance of the said accounts and thereafter, effect the necessary adjustments/corrections in the books.
We likewise recommended and Management agreed to the immediate remittance of amounts withheld to the concerned government agencies and ensure that monthly remittances correspond to the exact amounts withheld to avoid incurrence of penalties and surcharges and so that the Municipal officials and employees could enjoy the benefits due them.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the MT to furnish the Municipal Accountant a certified list of taxes due and collectible based on RPT Account Register/Taxpayer’s index card at the beginning of each year as basis in establishing the correct RPT and SET receivables.
Also, we recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant:
Maintain and update subsidiary ledgers of RPT and SET Receivables and reconcile the same with the records of the MTand effect the necessary adjustments in the books to come up with accurate and reliable RPT and SET Receivable accounts balance; and
Properly account the discounts based on the sharing prescribed under the Local Government Code and record fines and penalties charged on delinquent RPT and SET using the Tax Revenue - Fines and Penalties - Property  Taxes account (4-01-05-020).

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to:
Reconcile all dormant accounts and take appropriate actions to present a valid and correct balances of the affected accounts in the FS;
Request for authority to write-off dormant receivables accounts to COA subject to the requirements/procedures prescribed by the aforementioned Circular; and
Conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis and validation of the existence of receivable accounts to ensure that only valid receivables are recorded in the books.

We recommended and Management agreed to strictly comply with the Disaster Preparedness Minimum Standards developed by the LGA of the DILG and DepEd-DSWD-DILG-DOH JMC No. 1, Series of 2013, by taking into consideration the following:
Equip the MDRRMO personnel and volunteer with necessary trainings that will help them in the efficient and effective management of DRRM-related projects and activities;
Allocate funds in its LDRRM budget for the construction of additional evacuation centers to address the shortage; and
Maintain sufficient stockpile of relief goods.
We commended the MDRRMO for its notable work accomplished and we recommend to continue the work with enthusiasm in compliance with Section 12 of RA No. 10121.
We commended Management in filling-up the mandatory position of MDRRMO as required in NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-1.

We reiterated our prior years’ audit recommendation that Management require the Municipal Accountant and the Store keeper to intensify the review and reconciliation of their records on PPE to come up with reconciled balance of these accounts.

We recommended that Management facilitate the immediate disposal of the aforementioned unserviceable properties to recoup any salvage value thereof. Specifically, we recommend the following measures:
Conduct physical inspection and appraisal of the unserviceable properties and waste materials and furnish the auditor copies of fully accomplished/verified IIRUPs; and
Dispose the unserviceable properties based on the most appropriate mode of disposal pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular No. 92-386.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant to strengthen accounting controls over Cash.  The unrecorded disbursements/deposits be booked up in the General Journal by drawing a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV). This is necessary to take up the appropriate adjusting entries for all reconciling items noted in the BRS. These shall be supported with complete and proper documents;
Require the Municipal Accountant to coordinate closely with the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to ensure that all disbursements/deposits for a given period are booked up and recorded in the books of the Municipality.    In addition, bank confirmation be made regularly to validate actual existence of the accounts; and
Request from concerned depository banks, copies of the debit and credit memos to determine their validity and effect the necessary adjustments to correct the affected accounts balance.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to carefully check the entries made in the subsidiary ledgers to determine the cause/s of the discrepancy/ies and immediately make the necessary correcting entries to bring both accounts in agreement and to ensure the correctness and accuracy of the financial statements.

We recommended that the LSB strictly comply with the requirements of Section 272 of RA No. 7160 and DepEd, DBM and DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017 in identifying the PPAs that reflect an equitable distribution of the mandated priority expenses and refrain from using the SEF outside the scope for which it was established.

We recommended that Management strictly maximize the utilization of the 20 per cent DF and implement the PPAs within the targeted timeframe to achieve the social, economic and environmental outcomes essential in the attainment of the constitutional objective of a desired quality of life for all.

We recommended that the Municipal SWM Board:
Exert effort to expedite the approval of the 10-year SWM Plan 2015-2024; and
Exercise strong political will to implement the environmental laws on preserving and protecting the environment as mandated under RA No. 9003.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the:
Municipal departments Head concerned to ensure the prompt submission of the PPMPs to the BAC;
BAC Secretariat to act judiciously and be responsible in the consolidation of End Users’ PPMPs and APP and submit the report to the GPPB for publication; and
BAC to prepare the PMR, to be approved by the HoPE and to be submitted to the GPPB in compliance with Section 12.2 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that Management comply strictly with the aforementioned regulation and require the Municipal Engineer’s Office and other concerned officials to disclose all transactions involving PPAs for transparency and accountability.

We recommended that Management require the heads of the General Services Office (GSO) and Accounting Office to reconcile their respective records and further investigate the differences noted in the two reports and that adjustments if any, be effected in the books and RPCPPE.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require all collectors to:

a) reflect in the Report of Collections and Deposits the full amount received
and collected; and

b) remit the full amount of collections to the extent possible in adherence with
the pertinent provisions of PD No.1445, RA No. 7160 and COA Circular
No. 92-382.

We likewise recommended that management require the Municipal Treasurer
to observe the prescribed process for the receipt and verification of collections turned
over to her, especially at the end of the month, to ensure that all collections/income
are adequately accounted/recorded, and that no more than two (2) days unremitted
collection or P5,000.00 cash, as the case maybe, remain in the hands of the
accountable officer.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal
Treasurer to exert extra effort to reconcile their respective records; identify the
discrepancies and adjustments be effected in the hooks if warranted; and reconcile
records regularly in accordance with the abovementioned regulation.

a. Prepare and submit timely the BRS pursuant to Section 3.2 of COA
Circular No. 96-011 to provide the Audit Team enough time to examine the
said documents;

b. Conduct a thorough review of records to determine the validity of all
reconciling items and come up with the correct balance of Cash in Bank
accounts; and

c. Exhaust all possible actions in order to perform the reconciliations.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

* direct all the concerned accountable officials and employees to
liquidate/settle their cash advances immediately;

= direct the employees with shortages, loans amortization paid by the LGU
to reimburse the said payments either by paying cash or deducting from
their salaries or other remunerations immediately;

* require the Municipal Accountant to: (a) send demand letters to the
concerned accountable officials/employees to liquidate/settle immediately
their cash advances. Copy of the said demand letters bearing the receipts
of the concerned accountable officials/employees should be furnished the
Audit Team; and (b) adhere strictly to the provisions of Section 4.1.2 of
COA Circular No. 97-002 of not processing Disbursement Vouchers for
additional cash advances of Municipal officials and employees with
ontstanding cash advances; and exert exira effort to provide the schedule
of Due from Officers and Employees for Trust Fund and SEF to enforce
the liquidation/collection of the said account

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal
Accountant to exhaust all possible means to identify the causes of discrepancies in the
corresponding subsidiary ledgers and to effect immediate adjustment in the books of
accounts. Likewise, periodic reconciliation of the accounts should be made, to bring
both acconnts in agreement and to ensure acenracy and the reliability of the recorded
financial transactions.

We recommended that management require the Municipal Treasurer to
prepare a duly certified list of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the
year. The data should be based from the Real Property Tax Account Register
(RPTAR) or Taxpayer’s index card maintained at the MTO and furnish the
Municipal Accountant copy of the said list as the basis in recording the RPT
Receivable and SET Receivable in the books pursuant to provisions of the NGAs
Manual, Volume L

Also, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the overlooked
collection for the month June in the General Fund book.

‘We recommended that Management require, specifically the MGSO and the
Accounting Office to reconcile the RPCPPE and the accounting records and prepare
inventory report on the PPE under SEF and TF accounts. In order to do so, the
concerned agency officials should reconcile the differences noted in the two reports
and adjustments, if any should be effected.

‘We recommended that Management require:
a) the Municipal Accountant to provide the corresponding PPELCs and

RPLCs for Land, Power Supply System, School Buildings, Sports
Equipment and Construction in Progress account and to properly

update its property records for the appropriate reporting of all PPE in
the financial statements; and

b)  the Municipal General Services Officer to reconstruct the required
Property Cards based on the existing PPE records.

We recommended that Management secure insurance with the GSIS on all of
its insurable properties. Also, prepare an inventory report of the said properties and
submit the same to the Office of the Auditor to verify its correctness.

‘We recommended that Management create a Disposal Committee and conduct
inspection and appraisal of all the unserviceable properties and dispose the said
properties in accordance with COA Circular No. 92-386.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) ensure that ali
procurement be made through competitive public bidding to permit itself access to a
better chance of obtaining the most advantageous offer from a pool of qualified
suppliers. Aiso, in the event that procurement through competitive bidding becomes
impossible, we further recommended that the BAC adopt a modé of prociurement
that is appropriate to the procurement at hand, following the guidelines prescribed in
the above-mentioned provisions.

‘We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require:

1. The concerned Municipal Officials and Employees to properly document
their MILC and TLB;

2. Management should not process the claims for MLC and TLB if the Leave
Application Form is not properly filled out and the concerned personnel in
the HRMO verify the accuracy of the information stated therein;

3. The Municipal Accountant sign/certify the computation of TLB in
accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-001; and

4. The Municipal Accountant, HRMO and Municipal Budget Officer to
coordinate closely before processing the claims to attest the veracity and
validity of the expenses to be paid.

We recommended that in the future, Management first secure the MOA as one
of the requisites before the funds will be release to the beneficiaries. The MOA should
contain all the necessary provisions for the smooth implementation and liquidation of
the project or activities. We also recommended strict compliance with COA Circular
2012-001 on the proper documentations of all government transactions.

We recommended that Management:

a. require the BAC, the Secretariat and the TWG to strictly abide by the
standards set forth by RA No. 9184, its Revised IRR and other relevant
laws on government procurement;

b. require them also to exercise due diligence in the performance of their
duties to ensure that all requirements of the procurement law are faithfully
adhered to; and

c. direct the Municipal Accountant to observe the requirements of COA
Circular No. 2012-001 at all fimes to avoid suspension or disallowance in
audit,

In view of the observation noted, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor
require the Municipal Eugineer and the Bids and Awards Committee Chairman to
explain why the IPOW and the PhilGeps advertisement did not mention of tree
houses as stated in the MOA, which resulted in the completion of a project different
from what was committed in the MOA by the LGU of Bulusan, a patent violation
which might forfeit future access by the Municipality from GPBP Funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal
Accountant submit immediately to the Office of the Auditor the subject disbursement
vouchers, payrolls and checks pertaining to CY 2016 transactions to determine the
validity of these transactions.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer and the
Municipal Accountant exert extra effort in preparing and submitting on time the
subject accounts and financial reports including the supporting documents. We
suggest that such activities be properly monitored so that reports are submitted
promptly in accordance with the pertinent provisions of PD No. 1445 and COA
Circular No. 2009-006.
